wrong_options.A: WHERE filters rows based on conditions after JOIN has combined them. WHERE on its own doesn't combine tables.
wrong_options.C: GROUP BY groups rows for aggregation — it doesn't combine tables.
wrong_options.D: ORDER BY sorts results — no table combination.
wrong_options.E: SELECT retrieves columns from already-combined data — doesn't combine tables.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: JOIN = Connect two tables at the hip using a common column. Like matching students (table 1) with their courses (table 2) via student_id (the common key).
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Types of JOINs: INNER JOIN (only matches), LEFT JOIN (all left + matches from right), RIGHT JOIN (all right + matches from left), FULL JOIN (all from both), CROSS JOIN (all combinations), SELF JOIN (table joined with itself).
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: SELECT s.name, c.course_name FROM students s JOIN courses c ON s.course_id = c.id; — combines student names with their course names.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which SQL JOIN returns all rows from the left table and the matching rows from the right table?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: LEFT JOIN (also called LEFT OUTER JOIN) returns ALL rows from the LEFT table. For rows in the left table that don't have a matching row in the right table, NULL is placed in the right table's columns.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – LEFT JOIN – always includes all rows from the left table. If a left-table row has no match in the right table, NULLs fill the right-side columns.

wrong_options.A: INNER JOIN returns only rows where there is a match in BOTH tables. Unmatched rows from either table are excluded.
wrong_options.B: RIGHT JOIN returns ALL rows from the RIGHT table and matching rows from left — the mirror image of LEFT JOIN.
wrong_options.D: CROSS JOIN returns every possible combination of rows from both tables (Cartesian product) — no matching condition needed.
wrong_options.E: FULL JOIN returns ALL rows from BOTH tables, with NULLs where no match exists on either side.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: LEFT JOIN = LEFT table is the BOSS. All its rows appear, no matter what. The right table only adds info where it matches. No match? Fill with NULL.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Common use case for LEFT JOIN: Find all customers who have NO orders — SELECT c.name FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.id IS NULL;
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: LEFT JOIN vs INNER JOIN: If every left row has a match in right, they give same results. LEFT JOIN is safer — it never loses rows from the left table.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which type of SQL JOIN combines all rows from both tables, with NULLs where there is no match?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: FULL JOIN (FULL OUTER JOIN) returns ALL rows from BOTH tables. Where a row from the left table has no match in the right (or vice versa), NULL values fill the missing columns. It is essentially LEFT JOIN + RIGHT JOIN combined.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option D – FULL JOIN – includes every row from both tables with NULL where no match exists on either side. It gives the most complete picture of both tables.

wrong_options.A: INNER JOIN = only matching rows from both tables. No NULLs for unmatched rows — they are simply excluded.
wrong_options.B: LEFT JOIN = all rows from left, matching + NULLs for right. Only left side is fully preserved.
wrong_options.C: RIGHT JOIN = all rows from right, matching + NULLs for left. Only right side is fully preserved.
wrong_options.E: CROSS JOIN = Cartesian product — all combinations of rows. No NULL filling for unmatched — it combines everything.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: FULL JOIN = LEFT JOIN + RIGHT JOIN. Nobody gets left out. Every row from both tables appears, with NULLs where the other side has no partner.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: MySQL does not natively support FULL JOIN. Workaround: SELECT * FROM A LEFT JOIN B ON ... UNION SELECT * FROM A RIGHT JOIN B ON ...; — achieves FULL JOIN in MySQL.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: FULL JOIN use case: Finding records that exist in either table but not both — useful for data reconciliation. WHERE a.id IS NULL OR b.id IS NULL after FULL JOIN finds unmatched records.

dbms_exam_questions.question: In databases, which type of join returns all combinations of rows from two tables?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: CROSS JOIN returns the Cartesian product of two tables — every row from the first table is paired with every row from the second table. If Table A has 3 rows and Table B has 4 rows, CROSS JOIN returns 3 × 4 = 12 rows.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – CROSS JOIN – generates all possible combinations. No ON condition is used because every row is matched with every other row.

wrong_options.A: LEFT JOIN returns all rows from left + matching rows from right. Not all combinations.
wrong_options.B: RIGHT JOIN returns all rows from right + matching rows from left. Not all combinations.
wrong_options.D: INNER JOIN returns only matching rows from both tables based on a condition.
wrong_options.E: FULL JOIN returns all rows from both tables with NULLs for unmatched — not all combinations.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: CROSS JOIN = multiply the tables. 5 rows × 4 rows = 20 combination rows. Like getting all possible outfit combinations: 3 shirts × 4 pants = 12 outfits.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Cartesian product example: Customers (3 rows) × Products (100 rows) = 300 rows in CROSS JOIN result. Use with caution on large tables — can generate massive results.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: CROSS JOIN has a practical use: generating all combinations for scenarios like scheduling (all shifts × all employees) or test data generation.

dbms_exam_questions.question: In database systems, which operation is used to combine data from two or more tables based on a common related column?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: JOIN is the primary SQL mechanism for combining data from multiple tables based on related columns (typically primary key–foreign key relationships). It creates a combined view of related data.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option B – JOIN – combines tables HORIZONTALLY (adding more columns). The ON condition specifies the matching related column.

wrong_options.A: UNION combines query results VERTICALLY (stacking rows). It doesn't use related columns — both queries just need compatible column structures.
wrong_options.C: INTERSECT returns common rows from two queries — not based on related columns between tables.
wrong_options.D: CROSS JOIN combines all rows from both tables with no relationship condition — not based on a 'common related column'.
wrong_options.E: SUBQUERY is a query nested inside another query. It can achieve similar results but is a different mechanism than JOIN.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: JOIN = Joining tables at the waist using a matching key. UNION = Stacking tables on top of each other. Horizontal = JOIN. Vertical = UNION.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: JOIN is based on relationships (common columns). UNION is based on compatible structures. These are the two main ways to combine data from multiple sources in SQL.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Natural JOIN: Automatically joins on columns with the same name in both tables. Simpler but risky if column names match unintentionally.

dbms_exam_questions.question: In SQL, which clause is used to specify a condition while fetching data from a single table or by joining multiple tables?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: WHERE is the conditional filtering clause in SQL. It is used both in single-table queries and in multi-table JOIN queries to filter rows that should appear in the result set based on specified conditions.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – WHERE – specifies filtering conditions for rows, applicable whether querying a single table or multiple joined tables. In JOINs, WHERE further filters the combined result.

wrong_options.A: GROUP BY groups rows for aggregation — it doesn't filter individual rows.
wrong_options.B: HAVING filters aggregated GROUP BY results — not used for individual row conditions.
wrong_options.D: ORDER BY sorts results — no filtering function.
wrong_options.E: SELECT specifies which columns to display — not a conditional filter.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: WHERE = the question 'where does this row qualify?' — conditions must be met for a row to appear. Always comes AFTER FROM/JOIN and BEFORE GROUP BY.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: In JOINs: JOIN condition (ON clause) defines how tables are related. WHERE clause further filters the joined result. Example: FROM a JOIN b ON a.id = b.id WHERE a.status = 'Active';
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: WHERE vs HAVING recap: WHERE = filter rows (before grouping). HAVING = filter groups (after grouping). Both use conditions but at different stages of query execution.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which SQL keyword retrieves all records from one table and matched records from another table, filling unmatched fields with NULL?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: LEFT JOIN retrieves ALL records from the left (first) table and only matching records from the right table. Where the right table has no matching row, NULL is filled in those columns.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – LEFT JOIN – guarantees all rows from the left table appear. Unmatched right-side columns show as NULL.

wrong_options.A: INNER JOIN returns only rows with matches in BOTH tables. Left-table rows without a match are NOT included.
wrong_options.B: FULL JOIN returns all rows from BOTH tables — both sides are fully preserved with NULLs where no match.
wrong_options.D: RIGHT JOIN preserves all rows from the RIGHT table, not the left.
wrong_options.E: CROSS JOIN generates every possible row combination — no NULL filling based on matching.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: LEFT JOIN = the left table is the VIP — all its rows are invited to the result. Right table's rows only come if they have a matching ID. Crashers (no match) get NULL tags.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: A common pattern: Left table = main entity (customers), Right table = related data (orders). LEFT JOIN ensures all customers appear, even those who have never ordered.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Finding records in left table with NO match in right: SELECT * FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.id IS NULL; — finds customers with no orders.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which SQL keyword would you use to retrieve records from two or more tables based on a relation between them?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: JOIN is used to query data from two or more tables by specifying a relationship (common column) between them. It is the foundation of relational database querying — leveraging the relationships between tables.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – JOIN – is the keyword that connects related tables using the ON clause. SELECT e.name, d.name FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id;

wrong_options.A: UNION stacks rows from multiple queries — doesn't use inter-table relationships.
wrong_options.B: INTERSECT finds common rows between two queries — not based on table relationships.
wrong_options.D: MERGE is for conditional DML operations (upsert) between source and target tables.
wrong_options.E: INSERT adds new rows to a table — not a data retrieval operation.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: JOIN = Bridge between two tables. The ON condition is the bridge material (the matching key). Without JOIN, tables are islands. With JOIN, they connect.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Relational databases are designed around relationships. The power of a relational DB comes from its ability to JOIN tables efficiently using indexed keys.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: JOIN performance: Joins on indexed columns (like primary keys and foreign keys) are very fast. Joins on non-indexed columns are slow. Always index your join columns.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid SQL constraint?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: SQL Constraints are rules applied to table columns to enforce data integrity. They restrict the type of data that can be stored. GROUP BY is a SQL CLAUSE (for aggregation), not a constraint.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option D – GROUP BY – is NOT a constraint. It is a query clause used to group rows for aggregation. Constraints are defined during table creation (or with ALTER TABLE), not in SELECT queries.

wrong_options.A: UNIQUE is a valid constraint that prevents duplicate values in a column.
wrong_options.B: PRIMARY KEY is a valid constraint that uniquely identifies each row and prevents duplicates and NULLs.
wrong_options.C: CHECK is a valid constraint that validates column values against a custom condition.
wrong_options.E: NOT NULL is a valid constraint that prevents NULL (empty/missing) values in a column.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: SQL Constraints: NOT NULL, UNIQUE, PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, CHECK, DEFAULT. GROUP BY is not in this list — it's a query clause, not a rule for data integrity.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Complete list of SQL constraints: NOT NULL, UNIQUE, PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, CHECK, DEFAULT. Remember this list for IBPS SO IT exam.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Constraints can be defined: At column level (inline) or Table level (after all column definitions). Table-level constraints are needed for multi-column constraints like composite primary keys.

dbms_exam_questions.question: In SQL, which constraint ensures no two rows have the same value in a column?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: UNIQUE constraint ensures all values in a column (or a combination of columns) are distinct across all rows in the table. Unlike PRIMARY KEY, a UNIQUE column can contain NULL values (and in most DBMS, multiple NULLs are allowed).
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option B – UNIQUE – prevents duplicate values in the specified column. Each value must appear at most once.

wrong_options.A: CHECK validates values against a custom logical condition (e.g., age > 0). It doesn't prevent duplicates.
wrong_options.C: NOT NULL prevents NULL values but does NOT prevent duplicate non-null values.
wrong_options.D: DEFAULT provides a default value when no value is specified during insert. No uniqueness enforcement.
wrong_options.E: FOREIGN KEY ensures values in a column match values in another table's primary key — referential integrity, not uniqueness in the current table.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: UNIQUE = no twins allowed. Each value must be one-of-a-kind. UNIQUE allows NULL (one absence is okay). PRIMARY KEY = UNIQUE + NOT NULL.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: UNIQUE vs PRIMARY KEY: UNIQUE allows NULL values (in most DBMS). PRIMARY KEY does NOT allow NULL. A table can have ONLY ONE primary key but MULTIPLE unique constraints.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Composite UNIQUE: UNIQUE(column1, column2) — the COMBINATION must be unique. Individual columns can repeat as long as the pair is unique.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which database technique helps recover data after a crash?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: Checkpoints are a DBMS recovery technique where the system periodically writes all dirty (modified but uncommitted) buffer pages to disk and records the checkpoint in the transaction log. After a crash, recovery only needs to process transactions after the last checkpoint — making recovery faster.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option A – Checkpoints – help the database recover efficiently after a crash by providing a known consistent state to restart from. Without checkpoints, recovery would need to replay the entire transaction log from the beginning.

wrong_options.B: Deadlocks are a problem (circular waiting between transactions), not a recovery technique.
wrong_options.C: Locks are used for concurrency control to prevent conflicting access — not a crash recovery mechanism.
wrong_options.D: Paging is a memory management technique in operating systems — not database crash recovery.
wrong_options.E: Concurrency Control manages simultaneous access to data — it prevents issues during normal operation, not after crashes.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Checkpoint = save point in a game. When the server crashes, you don't restart from game start — you restart from the last checkpoint. Only transactions after the checkpoint need replaying.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Recovery process after crash: 1. Find the last checkpoint in the log. 2. REDO committed transactions after the checkpoint (WAL: write committed changes). 3. UNDO uncommitted transactions after the checkpoint (roll back partial work).
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) + Checkpoints together form the standard DBMS crash recovery mechanism. WAL ensures all changes are logged before being applied.
dbms_exam_questions.main_topic: SQL Constraints (Recovery topic misclassified in source)

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which database constraint ensures a column cannot have NULL values?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: NOT NULL constraint is applied to a column to prevent it from accepting NULL (missing/empty) values. Every time a row is inserted or updated, the NOT NULL column must have a value — it cannot be left empty.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option E – NOT NULL – directly prevents NULL values from being stored in the column. Any INSERT or UPDATE that tries to leave this column without a value will be rejected.

wrong_options.A: DEFAULT provides a default value to use if no value is specified — it doesn't prevent NULLs if NULL is explicitly inserted.
wrong_options.B: CHECK validates against a custom condition (e.g., age > 0). It can prevent some invalid values but doesn't specifically target NULLs unless you write CHECK(col IS NOT NULL).
wrong_options.C: PRIMARY KEY implicitly includes NOT NULL (PK = UNIQUE + NOT NULL), but PRIMARY KEY also enforces uniqueness. NOT NULL alone only prevents NULLs.
wrong_options.D: UNIQUE prevents duplicate values but ALLOWS NULL values (multiple NULL values are typically allowed in a UNIQUE column).

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: NOT NULL = this column is mandatory. Like a form field marked with * (required). You MUST provide a value — blank/NULL is not accepted.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Important: DEFAULT and NOT NULL can be used together. DEFAULT sets a fallback value so the column is never left without a value unless NULL is explicitly given.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: NOT NULL + DEFAULT combination: age INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 18 — if no age is provided, it defaults to 18. But NULL cannot be explicitly inserted.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which SQL constraint ensures that the value of a column must satisfy a specific condition before the row can be created?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: CHECK constraint enforces a custom logical condition on column values. Any INSERT or UPDATE operation that violates the CHECK condition is rejected. It allows you to define domain-specific rules beyond just data types.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – CHECK – validates column values against a developer-defined condition before allowing the row to be stored. Example: CHECK (age >= 18) ensures no row with age below 18 can be inserted.

wrong_options.A: PRIMARY KEY ensures uniqueness and non-null values for row identification — not a custom condition validator.
wrong_options.B: FOREIGN KEY ensures referential integrity (value must exist in another table) — not for custom conditions on the current column's value.
wrong_options.D: DEFAULT provides a fallback value when none is given — not a validation constraint.
wrong_options.E: NOT NULL prevents NULL values specifically — not a custom condition.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: CHECK = quality check. Before storing, it checks: 'Does this value pass the test?' If not, rejected. Like a bouncer checking age at a door.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: CHECK constraint examples: age INT CHECK (age >= 0 AND age <= 150), salary DECIMAL CHECK (salary > 0), gender CHAR(1) CHECK (gender IN ('M', 'F', 'O')).
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: In some DBMS, CHECK constraints can reference multiple columns: CHECK (end_date > start_date) — ensures end date is always after start date across two columns.

dbms_exam_questions.question: What does the following SQL query return? SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT department) FROM Employees;
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: COUNT(DISTINCT column) counts how many unique non-NULL values exist in the specified column. SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT department) gives the number of different departments in the Employees table.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option B – Total distinct departments – is correct. DISTINCT inside COUNT first gets unique department values, then COUNT counts them. If departments are: IT, HR, IT, Sales, HR — COUNT(DISTINCT) = 3.

wrong_options.A: Total employees = SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Employees; — no DISTINCT, no column specification.
wrong_options.C: Employees in each department = SELECT department, COUNT(*) FROM Employees GROUP BY department; — per-group count.
wrong_options.D: Average salary = SELECT AVG(salary) FROM Employees;
wrong_options.E: Minimum salary = SELECT MIN(salary) FROM Employees;

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: COUNT(DISTINCT col) = count unique values in col. COUNT(*) = count all rows. COUNT(col) = count non-null rows. Three different COUNT variations, three different results.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT department) answers 'how many different departments exist?' This is commonly asked in analytics: 'how many unique products sold?', 'how many distinct cities served?'
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Difference: COUNT(department) = count of rows where department is not NULL. COUNT(DISTINCT department) = count of unique department names. Always less than or equal to COUNT(department).

dbms_exam_questions.question: What does a primary key in a database ensure?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: A Primary Key is the main identifier for rows in a table. It has two fundamental properties: Uniqueness (no two rows can have the same primary key value) and Non-nullability (a primary key column cannot contain NULL). It is the backbone of relational database integrity.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – Each record is unique – is the primary purpose of a primary key. It guarantees that every row in the table can be uniquely identified. This uniqueness is essential for establishing relationships between tables.

wrong_options.A: Better memory usage is not a function of primary keys. Primary keys do create an index (which uses memory), but that's not their purpose.
wrong_options.B: Fast access to backup has nothing to do with primary keys.
wrong_options.D: Compression of data is not related to primary keys.
wrong_options.E: Sorting is faster is a secondary benefit of the index automatically created on primary key columns — but it's not the PRIMARY PURPOSE.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: PRIMARY KEY = National ID Card for each row. Just as no two citizens can have the same ID number, no two rows can have the same primary key value.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Primary Key Properties: UNIQUE (no duplicates), NOT NULL (no missing values), Only ONE per table, Automatically creates an index on that column.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Types of Keys: Super Key (any set that uniquely identifies), Candidate Key (minimal super key), Primary Key (chosen candidate key), Alternate Key (unchosen candidate keys), Foreign Key (references another table's PK).

dbms_exam_questions.question: In a database, which key is used to establish a relationship between two tables?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: Foreign Key is a column (or set of columns) in one table that references the Primary Key of another table. It establishes and enforces a link between two tables — creating the 'relational' nature of a relational database. This maintains referential integrity.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option D – Foreign Key – is the key that links two tables together. The child table's foreign key must match a value in the parent table's primary key — or be NULL.

wrong_options.A: Primary Key uniquely identifies rows WITHIN a single table. It is what the foreign key in another table REFERENCES.
wrong_options.B: Super Key is any set of columns that uniquely identifies rows. It's a theoretical concept, not used for relationships.
wrong_options.C: Candidate Key is a minimal super key — any column(s) that could serve as primary key. Not used for cross-table relationships.
wrong_options.E: Alternate Key is a candidate key that was NOT chosen as the primary key. It's still within the same table.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Foreign Key = Foreign Passport. It says 'I belong to another country (table)'. The employee table's dept_id is a foreign key pointing to the departments table.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Referential Integrity: The foreign key value must exist as a primary key in the referenced table. This prevents 'orphan records' — records referencing a non-existent parent.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: ON DELETE CASCADE: When a parent row is deleted, all child rows with that foreign key are automatically deleted. ON DELETE SET NULL: Child's foreign key is set to NULL when parent is deleted.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which normal form ensures that every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on every candidate key?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: Second Normal Form (2NF) builds on 1NF by eliminating partial dependencies. A partial dependency occurs when a non-prime attribute depends on only PART of a composite candidate key (not the whole key). 2NF requires that every non-prime attribute be FULLY functionally dependent on the ENTIRE candidate key.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option B – 2NF – specifically removes partial dependencies of non-prime attributes on candidate keys. A table is in 2NF if it is in 1NF AND every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on every candidate key.

wrong_options.A: 1NF requires: atomic column values (no repeating groups or arrays), each column has a single data type, each row is unique. It does NOT deal with partial dependencies.
wrong_options.C: 3NF removes TRANSITIVE dependencies — where a non-prime attribute depends on another non-prime attribute instead of directly on the key.
wrong_options.D: BCNF (Boyce-Codd NF) is a stronger version of 3NF — every determinant must be a candidate key.
wrong_options.E: 4NF deals with MULTIVALUED DEPENDENCIES — independent multi-valued facts stored in the same table.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: NF ladder: 1NF = Atomic values. 2NF = No Partial Dependencies. 3NF = No Transitive Dependencies. BCNF = Every determinant is a candidate key. 4NF = No multivalued dependencies.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Partial dependency example: Table(StudentID, CourseID, StudentName, CourseName, Grade). StudentName depends only on StudentID (partial). CourseName depends only on CourseID (partial). Grade depends on (StudentID, CourseID) fully. 2NF splits this into separate tables.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Functional Dependency (FD): X → Y means knowing X uniquely determines Y. Full FD: Y depends on ALL of X (composite key). Partial FD: Y depends on only PART of X.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which normalization form eliminates transitive dependency?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: Transitive dependency occurs when a non-prime attribute depends on another non-prime attribute, which in turn depends on the primary key. 3NF (Third Normal Form) eliminates these transitive dependencies, requiring all non-prime attributes to depend DIRECTLY on the primary key.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – 3NF – removes transitive dependencies. A table is in 3NF if: it is in 2NF AND no non-prime attribute transitively depends on the primary key.

wrong_options.A: 1NF only ensures atomic values and eliminates repeating groups.
wrong_options.B: 2NF removes partial dependencies (non-prime attributes depending on part of composite key).
wrong_options.D: BCNF is stronger than 3NF — it requires every determinant to be a candidate key (handles cases 3NF doesn't).
wrong_options.E: 4NF deals with multivalued dependencies, which are independent of transitive dependencies.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Transitive = A → B → C (indirect chain). 3NF breaks the chain: every attribute must point directly to the key. No middlemen allowed. Key → Non-prime only.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Transitive dependency example: EmployeeID → DeptID → DeptName. DeptName depends on DeptID (not directly on EmployeeID). This is transitive. 3NF splits into: Employee(EmployeeID, DeptID) and Department(DeptID, DeptName).
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: 3NF rule: For every FD X → Y in the table, either X is a superkey OR Y is a prime attribute (part of a candidate key). BCNF: For every FD X → Y, X must be a superkey (stricter).

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which normal form deals with multivalued dependencies?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: Fourth Normal Form (4NF) deals with MULTIVALUED DEPENDENCIES (MVD). A multivalued dependency occurs when one attribute independently determines multiple values of another attribute, and these are mixed in the same table with another independent multi-valued attribute — causing unnecessary data duplication.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option E – 4NF – specifically addresses multivalued dependencies. A table violates 4NF when it contains two or more independent one-to-many relationships. 4NF requires: no non-trivial multivalued dependencies unless the left-hand side is a superkey.

wrong_options.A: 1NF: Eliminates repeating groups and ensures atomic values.
wrong_options.B: 2NF: Eliminates partial functional dependencies.
wrong_options.C: 3NF: Eliminates transitive functional dependencies.
wrong_options.D: BCNF: Every determinant must be a candidate key — handles functional dependencies more strictly than 3NF.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Normalization ladder: 1NF (atomic) → 2NF (no partial FD) → 3NF (no transitive FD) → BCNF (every determinant is CK) → 4NF (no MVD) → 5NF (no join dependencies).
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: MVD example: Professor can teach multiple courses AND has multiple offices. Professor ->> Course and Professor ->> Office are independent. Combining them in one table (Professor, Course, Office) creates 4NF violation. Split into: (Professor, Course) and (Professor, Office).
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: 5NF (Fifth Normal Form) / PJNF: Eliminates join dependencies. Data cannot be reconstructed by joining smaller tables without spurious tuples. Highest practical normal form.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which concept in database design ensures minimal duplication of data by organizing fields and tables properly?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: Normalization is the systematic process of organizing a relational database to reduce data redundancy and improve data integrity. It involves dividing large tables into smaller, related tables and defining relationships between them according to normal form rules.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – Normalization – directly reduces data duplication by structuring tables so each fact is stored exactly once. This eliminates anomalies (insertion, update, deletion).

wrong_options.A: Denormalization is the OPPOSITE of normalization — intentionally adding redundancy for query performance. Used in data warehousing and reporting systems.
wrong_options.B: Indexing speeds up data retrieval by creating a sorted data structure on columns. It doesn't reduce duplication.
wrong_options.D: Joins are used to retrieve data from normalized (split) tables by combining them — they're a consequence of normalization, not the process itself.
wrong_options.E: Partitioning divides a large table into smaller physical segments for performance — it doesn't reduce data duplication.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Normalization = making data lean. No fat (redundancy). Every piece of information has ONE home. Denormalization = intentionally adding fat for speed.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Benefits of Normalization: Eliminates redundancy, prevents anomalies, improves data integrity, reduces storage. Disadvantage: May require more JOINs to retrieve complete information (performance trade-off).
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Real-world example: Instead of storing customer address in every order row, normalize: Customer(CustomerID, Address) and Order(OrderID, CustomerID). Address stored once, referenced many times.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Why is B+ Tree preferred in databases over Binary Search Trees?
options: A. B+ Trees are balanced and disk friendly
B. B+ Trees have no leaf nodes
D. B+ Trees require no memory
E. B+ Trees use more RAM
dbms_exam_questions.correct_answer: A. B+ Trees are balanced and disk-friendly
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: B+ Trees are the most widely used indexing structure in databases. They are always balanced (same depth from root to any leaf), have high fanout (many children per node), and store all actual data in leaf nodes which are linked together. This design minimizes disk I/O — critical for database performance.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option A – B+ Trees are balanced and disk-friendly – is correct because: Balanced = same time for any search. Disk-friendly = designed to fit in disk blocks (pages), minimizing expensive disk reads.

wrong_options.B: B+ Trees DO have leaf nodes — and actually store all data records AT the leaf level. This is a defining feature of B+ Trees (unlike B-Trees).
wrong_options.C: B+ Trees actually support BETTER sorting than BSTs for database use because leaf nodes are linked sequentially for range queries. BSTs are not optimized for disk.
wrong_options.D: B+ Trees do require memory (like any data structure) — this is false.
wrong_options.E: B+ Trees actually USE LESS RAM per level because their high fanout means fewer levels — accessing disk less frequently.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: B+ Tree = Best tree for Balanced, Bulk disk reads. All data at leaf level. Leaves linked like a linked list for range scans. Perfect for 'find all records where age BETWEEN 20 AND 30'.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: B-Tree vs B+ Tree: B-Tree stores data in ALL nodes (internal + leaf). B+ Tree stores data ONLY in leaf nodes. B+ Tree leaves are linked — supporting efficient sequential access and range queries.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Why B+ Trees beat BSTs for databases: BST height = O(log₂ N) — can be thousands of levels for large data. B+ Tree height = O(log_M N) where M is the order (hundreds) — only 3-4 levels for millions of records, meaning 3-4 disk accesses.

dbms_exam_questions.question: In databases, what is the purpose of an index?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: An Index is a database object that improves the speed of data retrieval (SELECT queries) on a table. It works like a book's index — instead of scanning every page (full table scan), you jump directly to the relevant location. Indexes use data structures like B+ Trees or Hash Tables.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – To speed up searches – is the primary purpose of database indexes. Queries on indexed columns are dramatically faster, especially on large tables.

wrong_options.A: Data integrity is ensured by constraints (PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, CHECK, NOT NULL). Indexes are for performance, not integrity.
wrong_options.B: Indexes INCREASE storage (they create extra data structures) — this is a cost, not a benefit.
wrong_options.D: Transaction management is handled by ACID properties, concurrency control, and TCL commands.
wrong_options.E: Encryption is a security feature — indexes have nothing to do with encryption.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Index = Book index = Skip to the right page. Without index = full table scan (read every page). With index = jump directly to results. Speed gain on large tables is enormous.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Index trade-off: Faster SELECT (benefit) but slower INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE (cost, because index must be updated). Extra disk storage required. Best used on frequently queried columns with high selectivity.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Types of indexes: Primary Index (on primary key), Secondary Index (on non-key attributes), Clustered Index (data rows physically sorted), Non-Clustered Index (separate structure pointing to data).

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which database indexing method allows multiple values from a single field to point to the same index entry, optimizing search queries?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: Bitmap Index uses a bitmap (bit array) for each distinct value in a column. For each row, the bit is 1 if the row has that value, 0 otherwise. It is extremely efficient for columns with LOW CARDINALITY (few distinct values) — like gender (M/F), marital status, or category codes.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option B – Bitmap Indexing – efficiently maps multiple rows to the same index entry (value). All rows with value 'M' share the same bitmap entry. Queries using multiple conditions (AND, OR) on bitmap columns are resolved by simple bitwise operations.

wrong_options.A: B-tree Indexing is best for high-cardinality columns (many unique values like names, IDs) — range queries, sorted results. Not specialized for low-cardinality.
wrong_options.C: Hash Indexing uses a hash function to map values to buckets. Best for exact-match lookups (= operator). Not for range queries or low-cardinality optimization.
wrong_options.D: Dense Indexing has one index entry per data record — every record is directly referenced.
wrong_options.E: Sparse Indexing has one index entry per data BLOCK, not per record — requires some linear scan within blocks.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Bitmap Index = Grid of 0s and 1s. Column 'Gender' has two bitmaps: one for M (1000110010...) and one for F (0111001101...). Very compact and fast for AND/OR operations.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Bitmap Index is ideal for: data warehouses, OLAP queries, columns with few distinct values (gender, region, product category). NOT suitable for OLTP (frequent updates) because bitmap updates are expensive.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Bitmap AND query: SELECT * WHERE gender='M' AND marital_status='Married' — perform AND on M-bitmap and Married-bitmap. 1-bits in result = matching rows. Single pass, very fast.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which of the following databases is an example of NoSQL?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: NoSQL databases are non-relational databases that do NOT store data in traditional rows-and-columns table format. They are designed for scalability, flexibility, and handling unstructured or semi-structured data. MongoDB is a document-oriented NoSQL database that stores data in JSON-like documents (BSON format).
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option D – MongoDB – is a NoSQL database. It stores data as flexible JSON documents without a fixed schema, unlike relational databases that require a rigid table structure.

wrong_options.A: Oracle is a powerful RELATIONAL database management system (RDBMS). It uses SQL and stores data in tables.
wrong_options.B: MySQL is one of the most popular RELATIONAL databases. Open-source, uses SQL.
wrong_options.C: PostgreSQL is an advanced open-source RELATIONAL database. Highly standards-compliant SQL.
wrong_options.E: SQLite is a lightweight RELATIONAL database embedded in applications. Uses SQL and stores data in tables.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: NoSQL examples to remember: MongoDB (document), Cassandra (column-family), Redis (key-value), Neo4j (graph), HBase (column-family). All others in options (Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite) are SQL/relational.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: NoSQL database types: Document (MongoDB, CouchDB), Key-Value (Redis, DynamoDB), Column-Family (Cassandra, HBase), Graph (Neo4j, Amazon Neptune).
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: SQL vs NoSQL: SQL = fixed schema, ACID compliant, vertical scaling, good for complex queries. NoSQL = flexible schema, often BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent), horizontal scaling, good for big data and real-time applications.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which database model organizes data in a tree-like structure?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: The Hierarchical Database Model organizes data in a parent-child tree structure where each parent can have multiple children, but each child has exactly ONE parent. It was one of the earliest database models (IBM's IMS in the 1960s). Data is navigated from top (root) to bottom (leaves) through paths.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option B – Hierarchical Model – uses a tree-like structure. Think of an organization chart: CEO at top, VPs below, Managers below VPs, Employees at the bottom. Each node has one parent.

wrong_options.A: Relational Model organizes data in tables (relations) with rows and columns. Uses SQL. No tree structure — tables are connected via keys.
wrong_options.C: Network Model is similar to hierarchical but allows MULTIPLE parents per child (a graph-like structure, not strictly a tree). Uses pointers/links.
wrong_options.D: Object Model stores data as objects (like OOP) with attributes and methods. Used in Object-Oriented DBMS.
wrong_options.E: Distributed Model refers to a database distributed across multiple physical locations — a deployment architecture, not a data organization model.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Hierarchical = Hierarchy = Tree. CEO → VPs → Managers → Employees. Every child has exactly ONE parent. It's a strict tree, not a graph.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Database model evolution: Hierarchical (1960s) → Network (1970s) → Relational (1980s, dominant today) → Object-Oriented (1990s) → NoSQL (2000s+).
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Hierarchical Model limitation: Cannot represent many-to-many relationships (a child can have only one parent). Network Model overcame this. Relational Model solved it elegantly with JOIN operations.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which database model organizes data into tables with rows and columns, and uses SQL for managing the data?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: The Relational Model is the foundation of modern databases. Proposed by E.F. Codd in 1970, it organizes data into RELATIONS (tables) consisting of TUPLES (rows) and ATTRIBUTES (columns). SQL is the standard language for creating, querying, and managing relational databases.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – Relational Model – is described precisely: tables (relations) with rows (tuples) and columns (attributes), managed via SQL. This is the most dominant database model used today.

wrong_options.A: Hierarchical Model uses tree structures (parent-child), not tables. Predates relational model.
wrong_options.B: Object-Oriented Model stores data as objects with methods — used in OODBMS. Does not primarily use SQL.
wrong_options.D: Network Model uses graph structures (records and sets) with pointer-based navigation — predecessor to relational.
wrong_options.E: Document Model (like MongoDB) stores data as JSON/XML documents — a NoSQL approach, not table-based.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Relational = Tables = SQL. The three are inseparable. If you see tables + rows + columns + SQL = Relational Model (RDBMS). Examples: MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, SQL Server.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: E.F. Codd's 12 rules define a fully relational DBMS. Key concepts: relation (table), tuple (row), attribute (column), domain (data type), primary key (row identifier), foreign key (relationship link).
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: RDBMS examples: MySQL, Oracle, Microsoft SQL Server, PostgreSQL, SQLite, IBM DB2. These all implement the Relational Model and use SQL as the query language.

dbms_exam_questions.question: In databases, which operation retrieves specific attributes from a relation?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: Relational Algebra is the theoretical foundation of SQL. Projection (π - pi) is the relational algebra operation that selects specific COLUMNS (attributes) from a relation. It is the column-filtering operation — equivalent to SELECT column1, column2 in SQL.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option A – Projection – retrieves specific attributes (columns) from a relation. If a table has 10 columns and you need only 3, projection gives those 3 columns for all rows.

wrong_options.B: Selection (σ - sigma) in relational algebra retrieves specific ROWS (tuples) based on a condition — equivalent to SQL's WHERE clause. Selection = row filter. Projection = column filter.
wrong_options.C: Join (⋈) combines two relations based on a common attribute — equivalent to SQL JOIN.
wrong_options.D: Union (∪) combines all tuples from two relations — equivalent to SQL UNION.
wrong_options.E: Intersection (∩) returns common tuples from two relations — equivalent to SQL INTERSECT.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Projection = Project specific columns onto the screen. Selection = Select specific rows meeting criteria. Easy distinction: Projection = COLUMNS (vertical slice). Selection = ROWS (horizontal slice).
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Relational Algebra operators: σ (Selection/WHERE), π (Projection/SELECT columns), ⋈ (Join), ∪ (Union), ∩ (Intersection), − (Difference/EXCEPT), × (Cartesian Product/CROSS JOIN), ρ (Rename).
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: SQL equivalent: SELECT name, age FROM students WHERE marks > 80; — here name, age = Projection (specific attributes), WHERE marks > 80 = Selection (specific tuples).

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which technique ensures that data is consistently written to the disk in DBMS?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: Logging (specifically Write-Ahead Logging - WAL) is the primary mechanism ensuring data is consistently and reliably written to disk. Before any change is applied to the database, a log record describing the change is written to the transaction log. This log enables recovery — both REDO (replay committed changes) and UNDO (roll back uncommitted changes) after failures.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option B – Logging – ensures data consistency on disk by recording all changes. The WAL protocol guarantees that no change reaches the database before its log record is safely written to stable storage.

wrong_options.A: Buffering is a technique where data is held in memory (buffer pool) before writing to disk — it improves performance but doesn't by itself ensure consistency.
wrong_options.C: Normalization reduces data redundancy in schema design — it doesn't ensure disk write consistency.
wrong_options.D: Paging is an OS memory management technique (virtual memory) — not a DBMS disk consistency mechanism.
wrong_options.E: Fragmentation refers to data being spread across non-contiguous disk blocks — a storage efficiency issue, not a consistency mechanism.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Write-Ahead Log = Write the diary entry BEFORE doing the action. If you crash mid-action, the diary tells you what to redo or undo. No log = no recovery guarantee.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: WAL Principle: The LOG must be written to stable storage BEFORE the corresponding data page. This ensures: if crash before page write = undo from log. If crash after page write but before commit = undo incomplete transaction. If crash after commit = redo from log.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Log sequence: BEGIN TX → [log: UPDATE salary 50000 → 60000] → [apply change to buffer] → [flush log to disk] → COMMIT → [flush data page to disk]. Recovery uses the log to restore consistency.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which database command is used to permanently save changes?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: COMMIT is a TCL (Transaction Control Language) command that permanently saves all changes made during the current transaction to the database. Once a transaction is committed, the changes are durable — they survive system failures (Durability property of ACID).
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option A – COMMIT – finalizes and permanently records all transaction changes. It marks the successful end of a transaction. After COMMIT, changes cannot be undone with ROLLBACK.

wrong_options.B: SAVE is not a standard SQL command. SAVEPOINT is (creates a partial rollback point within a transaction).
wrong_options.C: APPLY is not a valid SQL TCL command.
wrong_options.D: EXECUTE (or EXEC) runs a stored procedure — not for saving transaction changes.
wrong_options.E: UPDATE modifies row data within a transaction — it's a DML command that changes data but doesn't commit it permanently.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: COMMIT = Commit to the change. Like publishing a book — once committed (published), it's permanent. ROLLBACK = un-publish (before the book goes out). After COMMIT, no rollback possible.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: TCL commands: COMMIT (save permanently), ROLLBACK (undo all changes since last COMMIT), SAVEPOINT (create a recovery point within a transaction), RELEASE SAVEPOINT (remove a savepoint).
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Auto-commit mode: Many tools (MySQL Workbench) run each SQL statement as an individual auto-committed transaction by default. In production, use explicit BEGIN TRANSACTION ... COMMIT/ROLLBACK for multi-statement transactions.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which database concept is used to recover a database to a previous state?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: ROLLBACK is a TCL command that undoes all changes made during the current transaction (since the last COMMIT or SAVEPOINT). It reverts the database to its previous consistent state. Used when errors occur or a transaction needs to be aborted.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option B – Rollback – recovers the database to a previous state by undoing uncommitted transaction changes. It is the mechanism for aborting a transaction and reversing all its changes.

wrong_options.A: Backup is the process of making a copy of database data for disaster recovery — not the same as ROLLBACK. Backup restores from an external copy, not from undo logs.
wrong_options.C: Transaction is the unit of work itself — it's not the recovery mechanism.
wrong_options.D: Restore refers to recovering a database from a backup file after a catastrophic failure — different from ROLLBACK which works within a transaction's scope.
wrong_options.E: Commit is the opposite of ROLLBACK — it permanently saves changes, not reverts them.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: ROLLBACK = Roll the car back to where it started. Undo everything. COMMIT = Park the car permanently. Once parked (committed), you can't roll back.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT name: Partially rolls back to a specific save point within the transaction. Only the work done AFTER the savepoint is undone.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: SAVEPOINT example: BEGIN; INSERT row1; SAVEPOINT sp1; INSERT row2; ROLLBACK TO sp1; — undoes row2 but keeps row1. Then COMMIT; — saves only row1 permanently.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which database term refers to the action of creating a logical backup by duplicating a database's structure and contents?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: Database Backup is the process of creating a copy of database data (and sometimes structure) at a point in time. This copy can be used to restore the database after data loss, corruption, or disaster. Backup = proactive protection.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option D – Backup – refers to duplicating a database's structure and contents for protection and recovery purposes. It is the primary data protection mechanism in database administration.

wrong_options.A: Restore is the process of bringing back data FROM a backup. Backup = creating the copy. Restore = using the copy. They are complementary but opposite operations.
wrong_options.B: Export typically refers to exporting data to a file format (CSV, SQL dump) for migration or sharing — similar to backup but used for data portability.
wrong_options.C: Replication continuously copies data from one database server to another (master-slave) for high availability and load distribution — not a backup process.
wrong_options.E: Snapshot is an instant point-in-time copy (sometimes at the OS or storage level) — similar to backup but at a different level of abstraction.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Backup = Insurance policy for your data. You hope you never need it, but when disaster strikes (crash, ransomware, accidental delete), your backup saves you.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Types of backups: Full Backup (complete copy), Differential Backup (changes since last full backup), Incremental Backup (changes since last backup of any type). Each balances storage space vs. recovery speed.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: 3-2-1 Backup Rule: Keep 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with 1 copy offsite. Industry best practice for backup strategy.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which type of database backup only saves the changes made since the last backup, making it faster but requiring access to previous backups during recovery?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: Incremental Backup saves only the data that has CHANGED since the LAST BACKUP OF ANY TYPE (whether full or incremental). It is the smallest and fastest backup but requires ALL previous incremental backups plus the last full backup for recovery — making restore more complex.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option C – Incremental Backup – backs up only changes since the previous backup (any type). Fast to create, small in size, but recovery requires chaining multiple backup files.

wrong_options.A: Full Backup copies ALL data in the database regardless of changes — slowest and largest backup, but simplest recovery (just restore this one file).
wrong_options.B: Differential Backup saves all changes since the LAST FULL BACKUP. Bigger than incremental (accumulates all changes since last full), but simpler recovery (full backup + latest differential).
wrong_options.D: Mirror Backup is an exact mirror of the source — real-time copy. Any accidental deletion is also mirrored (limitation).
wrong_options.E: Snapshot Backup is an instant point-in-time copy — typically a storage-level operation, not a traditional incremental backup.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: Full = everything. Differential = everything changed since last FULL. Incremental = everything changed since last BACKUP (any). Recovery complexity: Full (easiest) < Differential < Incremental (hardest). Speed of backup: Incremental (fastest) < Differential < Full (slowest).
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Recovery scenario: Full (Sunday) + Incremental (Mon + Tue + Wed). To recover Wed's data: apply Sunday full + Monday incremental + Tuesday incremental + Wednesday incremental. All pieces needed. With Differential: Full + latest Differential only.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Enterprise backup strategies often combine: Full backup weekly + Daily incrementals during the week. This balances backup speed (fast daily incrementals) with manageable recovery.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which SQL command is used to modify an existing trigger?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: A Trigger is a stored procedure that automatically executes in response to specific events (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) on a table. To modify an existing trigger in SQL (especially Oracle), you use CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER — which recreates the trigger with new code, replacing the old version.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option D – CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER – is the standard way to modify an existing trigger. It either creates the trigger (if it doesn't exist) or replaces it with the new definition (if it already exists).

wrong_options.A: ALTER TRIGGER is valid in SQL Server for enabling/disabling triggers (ALTER TRIGGER name ENABLE/DISABLE). But for rewriting trigger logic, CREATE OR REPLACE is used in Oracle.
wrong_options.B: MODIFY TRIGGER is not a valid SQL command.
wrong_options.C: UPDATE TRIGGER is not a valid SQL command. UPDATE is for row data, not database objects.
wrong_options.E: CHANGE TRIGGER is not a valid SQL command.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: CREATE OR REPLACE = smart create. If it exists, replace it with the new version. If it doesn't exist, create fresh. It's the safe, one-command solution for both creating and modifying.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: Trigger types: BEFORE trigger (fires before the DML operation), AFTER trigger (fires after the DML operation), INSTEAD OF trigger (fires instead of the DML — used on views). Triggers fire on: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE events.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Trigger use cases: Automatic audit logging (record every change with timestamp and user), enforcing complex business rules, automatic notification, cascading operations not handled by foreign keys.

dbms_exam_questions.question: Which technique prevents SQL Injection?
dbms_exam_questions.main_concept: SQL Injection is a critical security vulnerability where malicious SQL code is inserted into application input fields and executed by the database. The attacker tricks the database into running unauthorized queries. Prepared Statements (also called parameterized queries) prevent this by separating SQL code from data — user input is treated as data, never as SQL code.
dbms_exam_questions.why_correct: Option B – Using Prepared Statements – prevents SQL injection by pre-compiling the SQL structure and treating all user-provided values as pure data parameters. Malicious SQL embedded in input is treated as a string value, not executable code.

wrong_options.A: Hashing Passwords protects stored passwords from exposure (if database is breached). It doesn't prevent SQL injection attacks.
wrong_options.C: Dynamic SQL Queries are the CAUSE of SQL injection vulnerability — not the solution. Dynamic queries build SQL strings by concatenating user input, making injection possible.
wrong_options.D: File Encryption protects data at rest on disk. It does not prevent SQL injection attacks on the running database.
wrong_options.E: Web Server Firewalls (WAF) can help filter known attack patterns but are not as reliable as prepared statements. It's a defense-in-depth measure, not the primary prevention.

dbms_exam_questions.memory_trick: SQL Injection prevention: PREPARE first (template), then BIND user input as parameters. User input is NEVER mixed with SQL code. Malicious input like '1 OR 1=1' becomes harmless string data.
dbms_exam_questions.exam_note: SQL Injection example: Username input: admin' OR '1'='1. Dynamic query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin' OR '1'='1' — always true, bypasses authentication. Prepared statement: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username=? — '?' is a parameter, never interpreted as SQL.
dbms_exam_questions.related_concept: Other security measures: Input validation, stored procedures, principle of least privilege (DB users with minimal permissions), Web Application Firewalls (WAF), regular security audits.
